perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall survive for a period of 3 years from the date of disclosure of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall survive for a period of 1 year from the date of disclosure of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality term to 1 year could be insufficient to protect certain types of trade secrets, potentially conflicting with the reasonable measures required to maintain trade secret protection under state law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts law protects trade secrets for as long as they remain confidential and provide a competitive advantage. A one-year confidentiality term may not be considered a reasonable effort to maintain the secrecy of information that has a longer competitive value, potentially undermining trade secret protection. The specific case law defining 'reasonable efforts' can vary, but a short, inflexible term could be challenged.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: General Law - Part I, Title XV, Chapter 93, Section 42 Skip to Content General Laws Part I ADMINISTRATION OF THE GOVERNMENT Title XV REGULATION OF TRADE Chapter 93 REGULATION OF TRADE AND CERTAIN ENTERPRISES Section 42 Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G Section 42 Search Search the Legislature Search General Laws Go Directly to a General Law Chapter Section Go to General Law Skip to Content Print Page Prev ious Next Section 42: Trade secrets; definitions applicable to Secs. 42 to 42G [Text of section applicable as provided by 2018, 228, Sec. 70.] Section 42. As used in this section and in sections 42A to 42G, inclusive, the following words, shall unless the context clearly requires otherwise, have the following meanings: (1) ''Improper means'', includes, without limitation, theft, bribery, misrepresentation, unreasonable intrusion into private physical or electronic space, or breach or inducement of a breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to limit acquisition, disclosure or use of information; reverse engineering from properly accessed materials or information is not improper means. (2) ''Misappropriation'', (i) an act of acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or who has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (ii) an act of disclosure or of use of a trade secret of another without that person's express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret or (B) at the time of the actor's disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that the actor's knowledge of the trade secret was (I) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (II) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (III) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to limit its acquisition, disclosure, or use; or (C) before a material change of the actor's position, knew or had reason to know tha

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality term from 3 years to 1 year, which contradicts the requirement to take reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. Massachusetts law, as indicated in the Law Explanation and inferred from the provided snippet defining 'misappropriation' (Snippet 1 & 2), protects trade secrets as long as they remain confidential and provide a competitive advantage. A shorter, inflexible term like one year may not adequately protect information with longer-term competitive value, potentially failing to meet the 'reasonable efforts' standard necessary for maintaining trade secret protection.

perturbation.original_text: Any and all disputes arising under or related to this Agreement shall be adjudicated exclusively in the Commonwealth of Massachusetts.
perturbation.changed_text: Any and all disputes arising under or related to this Agreement shall be adjudicated exclusively in the courts of Boston, Massachusetts, waiving rights to a jury trial.
perturbation.explanation: Forcing arbitration and waiving rights to a jury trial is a violation of Massachusetts law. This is an attempt to force the relinquishment of rights that should be optional.
perturbation.law_explanation: M.G.L. c. 231C, § 5 details guidelines in relation to court and arbitration jurisdiction. A change that limits which courts have jurisdiction and forces jury trial waivers, can create an inconsistency.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a jury trial waiver, which the Perturbation Explanation states is a violation of Massachusetts law and an attempt to force the relinquishment of rights. The Law Explanation mentions M.G.L. c. 231C, § 5 relating to court and arbitration jurisdiction. Since the original text does not include this waiver, and the altered text adds it, this creates a direct conflict by potentially infringing on the right to a jury trial, and according to the Perturbation explanation violates Massachusetts law. The scraped snippets are missing, however the explanations and the change of text provides enough information to determine that a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party agrees that it shall take all reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure and unau- thorized use of the Confidential Information. Without limiting the foregoing, Receiving Party shall take at least those measures that Receiving Party takes to protect its own most highly confidential information and shall have its employees, if any, who have access to Confidential Information sign a nonuse and non-disclosure agreement in content substantially similar to the provi-sions hereof, prior to any disclosure of Confidential Information to such employees.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party agrees that it shall take measures it deems appropriate to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure and unauthorized use of the Confidential Information.  The Receiving Party is not required to have its employees sign any nonuse and non-disclosure agreement prior to any disclosure of Confidential Information to such employees.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for employees to sign non-disclosure agreements weakens the protection of confidential information and could be considered a failure to take reasonable measures to protect trade secrets, potentially conflicting with Massachusetts law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts law requires 'reasonable efforts' to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. Eliminating the requirement for employee non-disclosure agreements could be viewed as a failure to take such reasonable efforts, especially if the receiving party's employees have access to sensitive confidential information. Case law would be needed to determine if this specific change constitutes a violation, but it creates a plausible conflict.

perturbation.justification: The changed text removes a specific requirement (employee non-disclosure agreements) that serves to protect confidential information, weakening the 'reasonable measures' the Receiving Party must take, as indicated in the Perturbation and Law Explanations. While the snippets do not explicitly mention NDAs, they define 'improper means' as including 'breach of a confidential relationship or other duty to limit acquisition, disclosure or use of information' (Snippet 1 & 2). By removing the NDA requirement, the contract increases the risk of such a breach, thus contradicting the need to prevent misappropriation.

perturbation.original_text: All documents and other tangible objects containing or representing Confidential Information and all copies thereof which are in the possession of Receiving Party shall be and remain the property of the Disclosing Party and shall be promptly returned to the Disclosing Party upon the Disclosing Party’s request.
perturbation.changed_text: All documents and other tangible objects containing or representing Confidential Information and all copies thereof which are in the possession of Receiving Party shall be and remain the property of the Disclosing Party and shall be returned to the Disclosing Party within 180 days of the Disclosing Party's request.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the return of confidential information to 180 days upon request contradicts standard practice for protecting trade secrets and confidential information, making it harder for the disclosing party to control their data and potentially conflicting with the 'reasonable measures' standard required by Massachusetts law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Massachusetts law requires 'reasonable efforts' to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. The alteration to return the property promptly has been changed to return the property within 180 days. This delay makes it difficult for the original party to maintain its secrecy. If a breach occurred in that period, the disclosing party has little recourse.

perturbation.justification: The altered text introduces a 180-day delay for returning confidential information, contrasting with the original's 'promptly' return. This weakens the protection of trade secrets, as highlighted in the Perturbation Explanation, potentially conflicting with the requirement for 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy, as described in the Law Explanation. While the law snippets don't explicitly mandate 'prompt' return, they define 'misappropriation' and 'improper means' which the delay could facilitate, making it harder to prevent unauthorized use or disclosure of the information. This delay makes it more likely that a breach of confidentiality could occur without recourse.
